theme: The Client Who Changed How We Listen

linkedin.text:
A client once told me: "You delivered exactly what we asked for — but not what we needed."

I've shared that sentence with every new team member we've brought on since.

It happened early in UPE's history. We had executed the brief flawlessly — venue, agenda, production values, all of it. The client was technically satisfied. But the event hadn't moved the needle on what actually mattered to them.

The fault was ours. Not because we did anything wrong. But because we never asked the right questions.

We had treated a strategic business moment like a logistics project.

After that, we rebuilt our discovery process from the ground up. Every new engagement now starts with a conversation that has nothing to do with budgets or venues. It starts with: what is this event supposed to change? In behavior, in culture, in pipeline, in perception?

That shift is what separates event management from event strategy.

Sixteen years and 1,500+ events later, I still think the best thing we ever invested in wasn't a new production tool or a supplier network. It was learning to ask better questions before we touched a single checklist.

If you're an HR, marketing, or sales leader planning something significant for the next cycle, I'm happy to start with those harder questions — no pitch, no deck, just a conversation.

DM me directly if that sounds useful.

#EventProduction #MICEEvents #IncentiveTravel

instagram.text:
"You delivered exactly what we asked for — but not what we needed."

A client said this to us early in our journey. It stung. And it completely changed how we work. 🎯

Now, before we touch a venue list or a budget sheet, we sit down and ask the questions most briefs never include:
→ What does this event need to accomplish six months later?
→ Who needs to feel something different when they leave?
→ What's the real problem we're solving?

1,500+ events across 130+ destinations taught us one thing above all: great production starts way before the production starts.

Save this if you're about to brief an event agency — these are the questions to ask first. 💬 DM us if you want to think through your next event together.

#EventProduction #MICEEvents #IncentiveTravel

facebook.text:
Somewhere around event number 200, a client said something that rewired the way we work.

She didn't complain. She didn't threaten to leave. She just said: "You delivered exactly what we asked for — but not what we needed."

That sentence sat with us for a long time.

We had been so focused on executing the brief that we stopped questioning the brief. We confused compliance with partnership.

From that point on, we built a different kind of intake process. One that asks harder questions before a single venue is scouted or a single budget line is written. Questions like: what does success look like six months after the event? Who in the room needs to leave feeling differently than they arrived? What is this event really trying to fix?

Over 1,500 events and 16 years later, that uncomfortable moment is still one of the best things that ever happened to us.

The clients who push back teach you more than the ones who just say yes.

If you're planning something important for your team or your business in the next cycle — we'd love to start with the harder questions first. Drop a comment or send us a message.
